title: Turkey has survived the energy crisis. It still needs a more deliberate economic strategy.
gist: Charles Lichfield argues Turkey survived the fifteen-week Hormuz/Iran energy crisis—aided by gold gains and a 2023 turn to orthodox high-rate policy—but its structural energy-import dependence, subdued FDI, and an unresolved EU customs-union problem mean geography and 'nimble geopolitics' won't deliver a high-income transition; he warns Turkey must welcome but not become a platform for Chinese-owned manufacturing.

themes.theme: Energy dependence and the balance of payments

insights.claim: Turkey's energy-import dependence makes its current account highly sensitive to oil prices, and the Iran war's price shock forced the Central Bank to defend the lira in March and late May 2026.
insights.speaker: Charles Lichfield
insights.so_what: Turkey is a high-beta loser from oil spikes; energy prices are the dominant macro risk to the lira and external balance.
insights.quote: Turkey's current account is highly sensitive to energy prices given the country's still-high energy import dependency
insights.locator: Without much oil or gas available domestically

insights.claim: Turkey's reserves net of loans and swaps remain just above negative territory only because rising global gold prices lifted the value of its gold holdings.
insights.speaker: Charles Lichfield
insights.so_what: The reserve cushion is gold-dependent and fragile; a gold reversal would expose genuinely thin net reserves.
insights.quote: this is only because the value of Turkey's gold holdings has increased with global prices
insights.locator: At just under thirty billion dollars

insights.claim: Turkey's unique Gulf swap lines (Emirati riyal, Qatari dirham) require counterparty consent to convert to dollars and may have been unavailable at the crisis peak as Gulf states faced their own currency mismatches.
insights.speaker: Charles Lichfield
insights.so_what: Gulf swaps are a less reliable backstop than headline reserves imply—a hidden liquidity vulnerability.
insights.quote: Ankara must seek consent before converting its Emirati riyal and Qatari dirham balances into dollars
insights.locator: The second new challenge stemming from Hormuz is financial

insights.claim: Turkey's 2024 rate hike from 8.5 percent to 50 percent cooled real growth from 5.1 percent (2023) to 3.6 percent (2025), validating the post-2023 turn to orthodoxy.
insights.speaker: Charles Lichfield
insights.so_what: Quantifies the orthodoxy payoff in financial stability at the cost of growth; the trade-off shapes Turkish asset risk.
insights.quote: The sharp interest rate hike in 2024—initially from 8.5 percent to 50 percent—has delivered the hoped-for cooling of real growth
insights.locator: The sharp interest rate hike in 2024

insights.claim: The policy rate has held at 37 percent since January across three successive MPC meetings, with latest inflation at 32 percent, but the Iran war was used in May to revise the end-year inflation target up from 16 to 24 percent.
insights.speaker: Charles Lichfield
insights.so_what: An upward inflation-target revision signals the disinflation path is slipping; relevant to lira-carry and bond positioning.
insights.quote: revise the end-of-year inflation target upwards from 16 percent to 24 percent

insights.claim: High Turkish bond yields attract foreign investors who take fright at perceived domestic political risk and premature rate cuts.
insights.speaker: Charles Lichfield
insights.so_what: Foreign-flow fragility tied to political risk is the key swing factor for Turkish carry trades.
insights.quote: these tend to take fright whenever domestic political risk is perceived to be growing
insights.locator: High bond yields have attracted foreign investors

insights.claim: Record-high gross fixed capital formation in Q4 2025 was driven by state-led reconstruction and social housing, while net FDI remained subdued and even deteriorated in early 2026.
insights.speaker: Charles Lichfield
insights.so_what: Investment is state/construction-led, not productive private capital—weak FDI undercuts the high-income transition thesis.
insights.quote: Foreign direct investment has remained subdued and even deteriorated on a net basis in early 2026
insights.locator: Gross fixed capital formation hit a record high

insights.claim: The July 2024 HIT-30 incentive program spreads too broadly across EVs, batteries, and semiconductors and should concentrate on export-oriented sectors like defense, tourism, medicine, or construction.
insights.speaker: Charles Lichfield
insights.so_what: Industrial-policy dispersion risks diluting returns; sector focus is the variable that determines its success.
insights.quote: The choice of sectors, ranging from electric vehicles (EVs) to batteries and semiconductors, is a little too broad
insights.locator: Launched in July 2024, the HIT-30 incentive program

insights.claim: The $17 billion Development Road project with Iraq depends on the Strait of Hormuz staying open (still uncertain), while the Caucasus Middle Corridor gives Turkey some leverage to attract rail-infrastructure investment.
insights.speaker: Charles Lichfield
insights.so_what: Corridor-led development is hostage to chokepoint geopolitics; Hormuz risk directly conditions Turkish infrastructure bets.
insights.quote: the planned Grand Faw Port sits on the Gulf and would depend on the strait remaining open, which is still far from guaranteed

insights.claim: Under the EU customs union, when Brussels signs deals with India or Mercosur, Turkey must open its market on the same terms without reciprocal access, a structural disadvantage Turkey lacks the leverage to fix.
insights.speaker: Charles Lichfield
insights.so_what: The asymmetric customs-union exposure is a persistent structural drag and the key unresolved EU-Turkey economic issue.
insights.quote: Turkey must open its market to their goods on the same terms—yet neither is obliged to open theirs to Turkish exports in return

insights.claim: The EU's Industrial Accelerator Act is considering a battery/EV carve-out that has already led China's BYD to slow-walk its promised Turkey investment in favor of its new Hungary plant.
insights.speaker: Charles Lichfield
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence EU policy is already diverting Chinese EV FDI away from Turkey—watch the carve-out's final form.
insights.quote: This has already led Chinese carmaker BYD to slow-walk its promised investment in Turkey, privileging its new plant in Hungary instead.
insights.locator: But the EU is considering a very specific carve-out

insights.claim: Turkey should welcome Chinese capital but must avoid being cast as a platform for Chinese-owned manufacturing, since the perception alone could stall customs-union modernization.
insights.speaker: Charles Lichfield
insights.so_what: A revised stance on Chinese FDI; the China-platform perception is a strategic risk to Turkey's EU integration path.
insights.quote: Turkey should still welcome the capital, but it must avoid being cast as a platform for Chinese-owned manufacturing.
insights.locator: One revision is warranted to the report's call

insights.claim: Turkey's new Emissions Trading Scheme should reduce or zero out what most Turkish sectors pay under the EU's Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism when certificate purchases begin next year.
insights.speaker: Charles Lichfield
insights.so_what: A domestic ETS as a CBAM hedge is a concrete competitiveness move for Turkish exporters to the EU.
insights.quote: its new Emissions Trading Scheme, which should make it reduce or zero out what most Turkish sectors will need to pay
insights.locator: Turkey has also been doing its homework

insights.claim: Geopolitical positioning will not make things fall into Turkey's lap economically: whatever 'land bridge' leverage the Hormuz crisis offered was more than offset by energy-price damage to the current account, lira, and inflation.
insights.speaker: Charles Lichfield
insights.so_what: The core call: don't price Turkish geography as upside—structural disadvantages dominate nimble geopolitics.
insights.quote: geopolitical positioning won't make things fall into Turkey's lap economically
insights.locator: An Atlantic Council report last autumn argued
